frameworks.name: ideal-observer / Bayes-optimal inference
frameworks.one_line: optimal learner weights prior and evidence by their respective reliabilities

frameworks.name: hierarchical Bayesian inference
frameworks.one_line: disentangles distinct sources of uncertainty across nested levels

frameworks.name: delta-rule learning with constant learning rate
frameworks.one_line: classical reinforcement-style update shown to be suboptimal under volatility

core_question: Does the human brain's learning algorithm implement confidence weighting and hierarchical inference when transition probabilities change unpredictably?
key_assumptions: observers combine prior and likelihood by their reliabilities
underlying transition probabilities can change at unpredicted moments
subjective confidence can be reported on a meaningful scale
manipulations: transition probabilities that change at unpredicted change-points
elicitation of subjective confidence about learned probabilities
key_findings: subjective confidence reports tracked ideal-observer predictions
subjects attached distinct confidence levels to each learned transition probability
distinct brain regions tracked observation likelihood and prediction confidence
right inferior frontal gyrus combined both signals consistent with confidence weighting
interpretations: the brain's learning algorithm relies on confidence weighting rather than a fixed learning rate
right inferior frontal gyrus implements a hierarchical process separating sources of uncertainty
